case_name: K. Surappa vs V. Venkatesha Reddy And Others
compensation_amount: Rs.20,000/- (plus costs and interest at 12% p.a. from date of petition); insurer to pay Rs.5,000/- and owner to pay the balance; advocates fee Rs.2,000/-

compensation_breakdown.interest: 12% per annum from the date of filing of the petition till date of payment
compensation_breakdown.notes: Insurer to pay Rs.5,000/- with proportionate interest and costs; owner to pay the balance; advocate's fee Rs.2,000/-; claim against other respondents rejected.

facts: The petitioner, a police constable deputed for election duty, was returning on 7-3-1971 at about 9:30 p.m. in bus No. MYY 3122 (owned by first respondent) when the bus dashed against a roadside tree and a temple and capsized. The petitioner alleged rash/negligent driving by the bus driver and sustained compound fracture of both bones of the left leg and another simple fracture. The bus had been requisitioned under Section 160 of the Representation of the People Act for election purposes; respondents included the owner, the Deputy Commissioner (who requisitioned the vehicle), the Election Commission, the insurer and the Union of India. The Tribunal initially dismissed the claim on liability grounds despite a finding of negligence; this appeal followed.
arguments_claimant: The claimant argued that requisitioning under the R.P. Act did not suspend the master-servant relationship between the owner and his driver and therefore the owner remained vicariously liable. He contended the owner was entitled to hire/fare under the R.P. Act and that the driver continued in the owner's employment; further, sovereign immunity could not absolve liability in these circumstances.
arguments_respondent: The owner argued the vehicle was not in his possession at the time and had been commandeered by the Deputy Commissioner, making the Deputy Commissioner the de-facto owner and employer of the driver. The Deputy Commissioner denied negligence and raised limitations and necessary-party objections; the insurer pleaded mechanical defect and contended that commandeering for election purposes was outside the policy cover.
ratio_decidendi: The Court held that "The mere fact that the vehicle ' is requisitioned by the Deputy Commissioner under the above statutory provision, does not lead to the inference that the driver of the vehicle ceased to be the employee of the owner nor does it indicate that the owner ceased to have interest in the vehicle." The court explained that "S. 160 only contemplates requisitioning the vehicle and not the services of the driver" and that "When the vehicle along with the driver is taken on hire ... the driver does not cease to be the employee of the owner." The burden rests on the owner to prove cessation of the master-servant relationship; absent such proof, the owner remains vicariously liable for the driver's negligence. Applying these principles, the court held the Tribunal's finding of suspension of the master-servant relationship could not be sustained and therefore the owner was liable for compensation.
final_order: Appeal allowed with costs; the Tribunal's dismissal set aside. Petition allowed in part awarding Rs.20,000/- with current interest at 12% p.a. from date of filing; insurer to pay Rs.5,000/- with proportionate interest and costs and the balance payable by the owner. Claim against other respondents rejected. Advocate's fee Rs.2,000/-. Insurer to pay costs of the appeal.
summary: This is an appeal by a police constable injured in a 1971 bus accident while the bus was requisitioned for election duty. The key issue was whether requisitioning suspended the master-servant relationship such that the owner and insurer were not liable. The High Court held requisitioning the vehicle under the R.P. Act does not, by itself, terminate the employer-employee relationship and therefore the owner remained vicariously liable; the insurer was liable up to policy limits. The court awarded Rs.20,000 with 12% interest, apportioned Rs.5,000 to the insurer and the balance to the owner.
embedding_summary: K. Surappa v. V. Venkatesha Reddy & Ors., High Court of Karnataka, 17 November 1995 (AIR1996KANT258; 1997 ACJ 482). Accident on 7-3-1971 at Harihar involving bus No. MYY 3122 requisitioned under Section 160 Representation of the People Act; claimant (police constable) sustained compound fracture of left leg and a simple fracture. Core legal issues: whether requisitioning suspends master-servant relationship; vicarious liability of owner; insurer's liability under policy (S.96(2) Motor Vehicles Act noted). Court held requisitioning the vehicle does not transfer the driver's employment and owner remains vicariously liable; insurer liable up to policy limits (insurer ordered to pay Rs.5,000/-), owner to pay balance; total compensation Rs.20,000/- with 12% p.a. interest from filing. Authorities relied on include Mersey Docks & Harbour Board v. Coggins & Griffith and Bhoomidas v. Port of Singapore Authority; statutory references include Section 160 and Section 161(2) R.P. Act and Section 96(2) Motor Vehicles Act. Practical outcome: owner and insurer jointly liable (insurer limited to policy cap), Tribunal order set aside and partial relief granted to claimant.
legal_principles: Requisitioning a vehicle under Section 160 R.P. Act does not, by itself, terminate the master-servant relationship between owner and driver
When a vehicle with its driver is taken on hire the driver ordinarily remains the employee of the owner unless the owner proves transfer of control over the method of work
Burden rests on the owner to establish cessation of master-servant relationship to escape vicarious liability
Insurer's defence that commandeering for election purposes excludes cover is not permissible under Section 96(2) of the Motor Vehicles Act (insurer liable subject to policy limits)
Where delay in obtaining compensation is excessive, court may award higher interest (here 12% p.a.)
sections_cited: Section 160 Representation of the People Act
Section 161(2) Representation of the People Act
cases_cited: Mersey Docks and Harbour Board v. Coggins & Griffith (Liverpool) Ltd., (1946) 2 All ER 346
Bhoomidas v Port of Singapore Authority, (1978) 1 All ER 956 (PC)
bm25_text: K. Surappa vs V. Venkatesha Reddy And Others High Court of Karnataka 1995 The petitioner, a police constable deputed for election duty, was returning on 7-3-1971 at about 9:30 p.m. in bus No. MYY 3122 (owned by first respondent) when the bus dashed against a roadside tree and a temple and capsized. The petitioner alleged rash/negligent driving by the bus driver and sustained compound fracture of both bones of the left leg and another simple fracture. The bus had been requisitioned under Section 160 of the Representation of the People Act for election purposes; respondents included the owner, the Deputy Commissioner (who requisitioned the vehicle), the Election Commission, the insurer and the Union of India. The Tribunal initially dismissed the claim on liability grounds despite a finding of negligence; this appeal followed. The claimant argued that requisitioning under the R.P. Act did not suspend the master-servant relationship between the owner and his driver and therefore the owner remained vicariously liable. He contended the owner was entitled to hire/fare under the R.P. Act and that the driver continued in the owner's employment; further, sovereign immunity could not absolve liability in these circumstances. The owner argued the vehicle was not in his possession at the time and had been commandeered by the Deputy Commissioner, making the Deputy Commissioner the de-facto owner and employer of the driver. The Deputy Commissioner denied negligence and raised limitations and necessary-party objections; the insurer pleaded mechanical defect and contended that commandeering for election purposes was outside the policy cover. The Court held that "The mere fact that the vehicle ' is requisitioned by the Deputy Commissioner under the above statutory provision, does not lead to the inference that the driver of the vehicle ceased to be the employee of the owner nor does it indicate that the owner ceased to have interest in the vehicle." The court explained that "S. 160 only contemplates requisitioning the vehicle and not the services of the driver" and that "When the vehicle along with the driver is taken on hire ... the driver does not cease to be the employee of the owner." The burden rests on the owner to prove cessation of the master-servant relationship; absent such proof, the owner remains vicariously liable for the driver's negligence. Applying these principles, the court held the Tribunal's finding of suspension of the master-servant relationship could not be sustained and therefore the owner was liable for compensation. Appeal allowed with costs; the Tribunal's dismissal set aside. Petition allowed in part awarding Rs.20,000/- with current interest at 12% p.a. from date of filing; insurer to pay Rs.5,000/- with proportionate interest and costs and the balance payable by the owner. Claim against other respondents rejected. Advocate's fee Rs.2,000/-. Insurer to pay costs of the appeal. This is an appeal by a police constable injured in a 1971 bus accident while the bus was requisitioned for election duty. The key issue was whether requisitioning suspended the master-servant relationship such that the owner and insurer were not liable. The High Court held requisitioning the vehicle under the R.P. Act does not, by itself, terminate the employer-employee relationship and therefore the owner remained vicariously liable; the insurer was liable up to policy limits. The court awarded Rs.20,000 with 12% interest, apportioned Rs.5,000 to the insurer and the balance to the owner. K. Surappa v. V. Venkatesha Reddy & Ors., High Court of Karnataka, 17 November 1995 (AIR1996KANT258; 1997 ACJ 482). Accident on 7-3-1971 at Harihar involving bus No. MYY 3122 requisitioned under Section 160 Representation of the People Act; claimant (police constable) sustained compound fracture of left leg and a simple fracture. Core legal issues: whether requisitioning suspends master-servant relationship; vicarious liability of owner; insurer's liability under policy (S.96(2) Motor Vehicles Act noted). Court held requisitioning the vehicle does not transfer the driver's employment and owner remains vicariously liable; insurer liable up to policy limits (insurer ordered to pay Rs.5,000/-), owner to pay balance; total compensation Rs.20,000/- with 12% p.a. interest from filing. Authorities relied on include Mersey Docks & Harbour Board v. Coggins & Griffith and Bhoomidas v. Port of Singapore Authority; statutory references include Section 160 and Section 161(2) R.P. Act and Section 96(2) Motor Vehicles Act. Practical outcome: owner and insurer jointly liable (insurer limited to policy cap), Tribunal order set aside and partial relief granted to claimant. K.
